I think this is an issue that needs serious thinking even if
SSL does not support duplex operation. (To be confirmed: Is it so or not?)

Suppose we have an application running full-duplex on
normal sockets. Now, we just want to deploy it in a SSL/TLS
setup. You can not do it without serious restructuring.

Even worse, if it is dominantly built around full-duplex logic
then it will have no chance of running under SSL.

I think a SSL duplex adapter/emulator is necessary somewhere.

On 3/16/17, Nathaniel J. Smith <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> Of course SSL supports full duplex operation.

Probably it is. But a quick search does not provide any
good docs about it. There must be some read-write
sequencing for a thing that needs to read while writing or
needs to write while reading. Otherwise real reads and
reads related to write can mix-up.

And if there is a "sequencing" relation, we have to rethink
the meaning of "full" duplex. May be the libraries providing
that illusion. But with regard to spec and real traffic on the
tcp level I have doubts.

Anyway I'm not an expert on SSL/TLS so take my comments
with grain and salt. Any good pointers on the subject will be
appreciated.

In the meantime, you can use txdbus within an asyncio app using `asyncioreactor` and [`asFuture(loop)`](https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/ticket/8748) which are now merged in [trunk](https://github.com/twisted/twisted/pull/489):
```
import asyncio
from asyncio.tasks import ensure_future

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
asyncioreactor.install(eventloop=loop)

from twisted.internet import reactor, defer, task
from txdbus import error, client

async def show_desktop_notification():
    '''
    Displays "Hello World!" in a desktop notification window for 3 seconds
    '''
    con = await client.connect(reactor, 'session').asFuture(loop)

    notifier = await con.getRemoteObject('org.freedesktop.Notifications',
                                         '/org/freedesktop/Notifications').asFuture(loop)

    nid = await notifier.callRemote('Notify',
                                    'Example Application',
                                    0,
                                    '',
                                    'Tx DBus Example',
                                    'Hello World!',
                                    [], dict(),
                                    3000).asFuture(loop)

ensure_future(show_desktop_notification())
loop.run_forever()
```

Co-author of txaio here;)

Using txaio, the txdbus library could be made working on Python 2.7+ for both Twisted and asyncio from _one codebase_.

txdbus in its implementation would need to stick to:

1. no co-routines, only plain Deferreds/Futures (eg `txaio.create_future()` will produce either a Twisted Deferred or an asyncio Future depending on which framework was chosen using `txaio.use_twisted()` or `txaio.use_asyncio()`)
2. no use of Deferred chaining (as asyncio Futures don't support that in the way Twisted does)

Further, an internal protocol/factory abstraction is needed. Eg here is [AutobahnPython](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python), biggest txaio user:

* `autobahn.websocket.protocol.WebSocketClientProtocol` contains all network framework independent code
* then there are 2 classes: `autobahn.twisted.websocket.WebSocketClientProtocol` and `autobahn.asyncio.websocket.WebSocketClientProtocol` which have the network framework specific code
* these classes are the ones used by users, and they derive from 2 classes: WebSocketAdapterProtocol and the generic WebSocketClientProtocol
* the adapter class member function `WebSocketAdapterProtocol.dataReceived` (Twisted) or `WebSocketAdapterProtocol.data_received` (asyncio) then forward that to the generic `WebSocketClientProtocol._dataReceived` method

Granted, this is a bit of a tricky design, but it achieves the goal of minimizing network framework specific code, maximize agnostic code, and all from a single code base.

I believe this error is a result of a bug in distribute/setuptools prior to Distribute 0.6.24 (or maybe 0.6.26). I forget specifically what the issue is, but I'm pretty sure the fix is to upgrade Distribute to a late, stable version.

The underlying cause is not that keyring requires hgtools (it doesn't), but that it does require pytest-runner during setup, which in turn requires hgtools during setup, and that chain of setup_requires triggers errors in older versions of setuptools/distribute.

Unfortunately, the only thing keyring can do to fix the issue is to not have the setup requires dependency (which also means it can't have setuptools resolve its test dependencies when setup.py is invoked).

I apologize for the long time in addressing the bug report. Please let me know if you find that updating Distribute doesn't resolve the issue.

I added an abstract class RunPeriod that can be derived from to handle intraday time frames. Check out the code. If you wanted RunHourly, it would look something like the code below:


    class RunHourly(RunPeriod):

    """
    Returns True on hour change.

    Args:
        * run_on_first_date (bool): determines if it runs the first time the algo is called
        * run_on_end_of_period (bool): determines if it should run at the end of the period
          or the beginning
        * run_on_last_date (bool): determines if it runs on the last time the algo is called

    Returns True if the target.now's hour  or day has changed
    compared to the last(or next if run_on_end_of_period) date, if not returns False.
    Useful for daily rebalancing strategies.

    """
    def compare_dates(self, now, date_to_compare):
        if now.date() != date_to_compare.date() or now.date().hour != date_to_compare.date().hour:
            return True
        return False

Upon further consideration and experience actually using VCRpy with pytest, I have come to realize that while my initial motivation was perhaps trivial, not being able to easily use it as a pytest fixture is problematic because I cannot easily mix it with other fixtures, which might themselves make HTTP calls that VCRpy needs to intercept.

Examples:
* Testing Celery tasks with the [celery_worker](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/userguide/testing.html#celery-worker-embed-live-worker) fixture, where the tasks themselves make HTTP requests that need to be intercepted. The `celery_worker` starts a new thread/process, so the VCRpy recording needs to start before the worker is started.
* Performing cleanup before and/or after the tests would ordinarily be a fixture, but because I have to use VCRpy as a decorator (or context manager), the best I've been able to do is cleanup in a context manager.

Being able to use the built-in name generation is also essential, because having to explicitly specify the cassette name means that the fixture is not reusable, so I would need a separate fixture for every test.

Now that I've written all this, I'm looking over the VCRpy's own tests, which I see are also written with pytest, I see a potential near resolution: Make a pytest fixture, manually calculate the cassette path from the `request` fixture and yield the cassette from a context manager:

```python
@pytext.fixture(scope='function')
def my_vcr_recording(request, samples_path):
    cassette_name = calculate_cassette_file_name(request.function.__name__)

Not a bug in `requests`, and not one in Pyrebase either. The [Generic Python app](https://help.papertrailapp.com/kb/configuration/configuring-centralized-logging-from-python-apps/#generic-python-app) instructions in Papertrail's Knowledge Base are broken. They instruct you to add a filter to the root logger that adds a `hostname` attribute to log records, and a handler to the root logger with a formatter that uses the `hostname` attribute. However, a quirk of Python's logging module is that while a logger's handlers are invoked for log records logged by descendant loggers, its *filters* are not applied to such records. Consequently, with Papertrail's currently documented setup, any time that a child logger tries to log *anything* at a level equal to or higher than the root logger's log level, the formatter tries to use a `hostname` attribute that doesn't exist and everything explodes - as you've discovered. Since creating a child logger is a standard practice for libraries in Python, this bug renders Papertrail's "generic Python app" example setup incompatible with basically the entire ecosystem of Python libraries.

Fortunately, there's a trivial fix: instead of doing `logger.addFilter(f)` as shown in Papertrail's docs, do `syslog.addFilter(f)` - i.e. add the filter to the *handler* instead of the *logger*. I've just emailed Papertrail explaining this to them and suggesting that they make the change.

Hahah, I think you're seeing my bias of having a ton of data to play with. 

The approach you outlined is definitely theoretically possible, and when you're working with small datasets (like Kaggle competitions), that approach is a great way to maximize the amount of models you can create in a data-constrained environment. 

However, that approach adds a decent bit more complexity, both to the codebase itself, as well as how to reason about it. One of the big goals of auto_ml is to be able to explain it to relatively non-technical people pretty easily (designers, product managers, analysts, etc.). Using out-of-fold predictions across many predictors ensembled together this way significantly increases the explanation difficulty of auto_ml. 

It also makes other things difficult to do, like 
- proper automated ensembling of many estimators (which I hope to do in the relatively near future!)
- reasoning about the trained models and getting analytics from them
- saving and loading models in different environments while keeping the file size small (using cv=10 means we have 10x as many models to save and load and get predictions from)

It's a great idea, and that approach is one taken pretty consistently by people doing batch processing on relatively small (or even just fixed) datasets where accuracy is paramount above all. Honestly, for those use cases, [auto-sklearn](https://github.com/automl/auto-sklearn) or even [xcessiv](https://github.com/reiinakano/xcessiv) are probably the best, with their wild ensembling approaches. auto_ml is designed for production environments, where data is plentiful, file size and prediction time are important considerations, and explaining models (and their analytics) to different stakeholders is required for shipping new features. 

I really like the idea, and I strongly encourage you to keep submitting more ideas like this! I just don't think that at this time, this idea is feasible for this project. But I hope that explaining the thought behind this a bit more is helpful, and encourages you to keep submitting ideas! I've shot down ideas  at work dozens of times (like trying to interpret tree-based models in a linear-model way), before finally realizing they're possible in a much easier way than I or anyone else imagined, so I'm very open to being wrong on this or any other ideas that seem difficult or impossible at first.

@viniciusmayer as I said, this is likely not a result of any wordcloud processing, but a result of how you represent and store your text. As I said above, I'm happy to investigate if you provide code to reproduce.

And no, this is not really related to #70. #70 is about the text rendering being done left-to-right, and whitespace not being an adequate separator of words for arabic.
WordCloud makes a single assumptino about language, which is that white-space separates words. It also assumes that your system is setup correctly for your language, so that ``\w`` covers the alphanumeric range of your language.
If your language, like Spanish or Swedish or German or many others, fulfil the whitespace criterion, but you are having trouble, that is probably either because you chose a font that doesn't contain the letters you're trying to render, or you didn't represent the input text as properly encoded unicode.

proxy. The aiohttp-wsgi-serve command line interface should serve your

that aiohttp-wsgi does not support streaming responses. Streaming responses
are generally not needed in Python wsgi apps, but your app my be different.

It's less mature than waitress, but it's static file server is better than
serving files via the wsgi app.

Some general thoughts on adding a variable to determine if a given statement should be saved to the database when returned from a logic adapter:

My initial thought on this is that whatever is returned from a logic adapter should always be saved (I am open to alternate suggestions). My reasoning for this is that the response that a logic adapter returns is part of a conversation. In a conversation each statement and the order in which they occur is an important characteristic. Although there is nothing implemented at the moment that takes the context of the current conversation into account, it is my hope that there will be in the future. For each statement that exists in a bot's database, the most valuable statements are those in which the bot has learned which other statement(s) the given statement is in response to. This is because this data makes it possible to select a match to a user's input that is known to yield a response.

So if a user asks the bot a question, lets say something like "What time is it?", and the bot replies that "The current time is 10:59pm", we can only assume that whatever the user says next is a response to the bot's output. I would say that reasonably that would warrant maintaining the integrity of this data relationship by saving this data to the database.

Hm, good point. We do have `--text=-` or `--text -`, which lets you pipe the text into stdin. It's not particularly discoverable, though. You could run `wormhole send --text=-`, wait for the prompt, then type your message into stdin and hit Ctrl-D when you're done (you don't get an editor in that case, but I think backspace would still work).

I don't think our argument parser makes it easy to accept any of `--text`, `--text message`, `--text=message`. The former would be a flag (in [Click parlance](http://click.pocoo.org/6/parameters/)), the latter two would be string parameters, and I think Click can't accept both because it'd be ambiguous (is `wormhole send --text FILE` supposed to send F-I-L-E or ./FILE?). We might need to replace the argument parser, and/or require that if you use `--text`, it must appear at the end of the argv array (so it's not really an option, but rather it's a funny-named subcommand that takes an optional argument).

How weird would it be to use another special character to mean "please run an editor"? Like `wormhole send --text=@` or something?

OTOH I'm a little concerned about adding process-spawning to the program , and making it work properly on windows (feature creep / maintenance cost).. I'll have to think about it. I suppose this could be accomplished with a wrapper script, and we could put something in a contrib/ directory, but that would be even less discoverable.

The test does not work against httpbin.org now, it will response `411 Length Required`:
```python
>>> import requests
>>> requests.__version__
'2.18.4'
>>> 
>>> def stream():
...     yield b'ham'
...     yield b'spam'
... 
>>> 
>>> r = requests.post('http://httpbin.org/post', data=stream())
>>> r
<Response [411]>
>>> r.reason
'Length Required'
>>> 
```

If test against a local gunicorn server, `r.json()['data']` will be an empty string, the reason is werkzeug ignore chunked request body(`werkzeug.wsgi.py:get_input_stream`):
```python
    # If the request doesn't specify a content length and there is no max
    # length set, returning the stream is potentially dangerous because it
    # could be infinite, maliciously or not. If safe_fallback is true, return
    # an empty stream for safety instead.
    if content_length is None and max_content_length is None:
        return safe_fallback and _empty_stream or stream
```

Sometimes it will cause `requests.exceptions.ConnectionError: [Errno 32] Broken pipe`, the reason is requests still write date after the server close connection, the net traffic is:
```
time>>>---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------->>>
requests:  send request headers                                                           send chunked body ...
httpbin:                                          send response    close connection
```
If add `time.sleep(1)` before the yield of stream function, it will always cause `Broken pipe`.

Well I actually had problems while I was using this library - don't know where to get the summary log, cannot resume the previous (very costly)experiment when it stops accidentally, then I came up with the idea of this PR.

Maybe the description above is not quite sufficient to tell what is going on, I think I could be nice if I do some more explanations here...

1. Update on print_summary() - mainly in helpers.py
This function was originally not implemented. I was trying to resemble the style in the original code printing the 'Initialization' part and the 'Bayesian Optimization' part, especially the color and indention/padding style. I pass space.X and space.Y from bayesian_optimization.py for printing, then the results are sorted before print. I did not print the 'Step' and 'Time' though, since they would only make sense during the BO process.

2. Add database support - add new file db_manager.py, add new APIs to bayesian_optimization.py.
In db_manager.py, I use sqlalchemy to talk to the database. This is able to talk many databases, especially it supports SQLite - a lightweight database that does not require any configuration, which is simple for experiments. Of course, if someone already has had a database with configurations, just change the style of the connection string. They are all well written in sqlalchemy's doc. 

I did four functions inside db_manager.py - init_db, load, save and clear, they are wrapped as APIs in bayesian_optimization.py. The idea is that, using a pre-defined table called 'target_space_table' to store the data. 

The connection string is passed to the bo.init_db API as bo.init_db(conn_str), the database is specified after this.

Whenever the experiment is finished, if the user calls bo.save(), the database manager would obtain space.X and space.Y, then pickle them as objects, then save them into the database table. 
In this table, there is always 1 row, 3 columns,  since multi-dimensional data are pickled as objects. One column for primary key(id), one for space.X and one for space.Y.

If the user calls bo.load(), db_manager.load() would return objects for initialization, then they are added into the BO object as initialization information after calling bo.inititialze(init_objects) and bo.maximize(init_points=0, n_iters=0) .

The bo.clear() API just a way to remove all the previous data programmatically.

The usage including exception handling is written in examples/database_interactions.ipynb.


I also added a test for db_manager.py to assert that saving, loading, then maximizing same steps of the same function periodically would increase the bo initialization size regularly by the number of steps.


Hope that helps!

The version mapping's default behavior is to use same version with the system RPM's version on local.

`rpm-py-installer` gets the RPM version from a command `rpm --version` by below command, and build and install the version's Python binding from the source on local.

`rpm-py-installer` do not see the RPM packages `python[23]-rpm`.

## For detail.

1. Get the version string.

  ```
  $ rpm --version
  RPM version N.N.N.N (Version string)
  ```

2. Get the target archive tar.gz file from https://github.com/rpm-software-management/rpm/releases , or get source by `git clone && git checkout tag or branch`.

3. Patching `setup.py.in` into `setup.py`.
https://github.com/rpm-software-management/rpm/blob/master/python/setup.py.in

4. Build and install the Python binding by the file.


## Specifying the Python binding version

Install the version 4.14.0

```
$ RPM_PY_VERSION=4.14.0 pip install rpm-py-installer
```

Install from the edge of `rpm-4.14.x` branch in the `rpm` repository (https://github.com/rpm-software-management/rpm).

```
$ GIT_BRANCH=rpm-4.14.x pip install rpm-py-installer
```

Hello miguel,

After looking to some possible scenarios, i think i found something here. It only happens on a redis backend for the a certain background process to emit.  

Whenever a client is disconnected or timing out because of no ping/pong response, then here comes a delayed emit from the background process, it tries to send to the 'sid' that was disconnected. Then it dies because the socket is not there.

Here is the code from the socket.io/server.py

```python
 def _emit_internal(self, sid, event, data, namespace=None, id=None):
     """Send a message to a client."""
     if six.PY2 and not self.binary:
         binary = False  # pragma: nocover
     else:
         binary = None
     # tuples are expanded to multiple arguments, everything else is sent
     # as a single argument
     if isinstance(data, tuple):
         data = list(data)
     else:
         data = [data]
     self._send_packet(sid, packet.Packet(packet.EVENT, namespace=namespace,
                                          data=[event] + data, id=id,
                                          binary=binary))

```

Should we have something like:
```python
def _emit_internal(self, sid, event, data, namespace=None, id=None):
     """Send a message to a client."""
     if six.PY2 and not self.binary:
         binary = False  # pragma: nocover
     else:
         binary = None
     # tuples are expanded to multiple arguments, everything else is sent
     # as a single argument
     if isinstance(data, tuple):
         data = list(data)
     else:
         data = [data]
         try:
             self._send_packet(sid, packet.Packet(packet.EVENT, namespace=namespace,
                                          data=[event] + data, id=id,
                                          binary=binary))
         except:
             self._handle_disconnect(sid,namespace)
```


This should catch bad sending of packets and doesn't let the thread die handling the redis pub/sub events?

Gevent uses cooperative multitasking. That means that your functions need to release the CPU voluntarily to let other tasks run. In your background task, you need to call `sio.sleep(0)` often (such as once per loop iteration) to release the CPU to other waiting tasks.

Stylistically, I'm not convinced a tuple should be preferred over a list. Semantically speaking, the example is of unknown length of uniform elements. This is the intended use of a list, not a tuple.

See [Python's documentation on the matter](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#tuples-and-sequences):

> Though tuples may seem similar to lists, they are often used in different situations and for different purposes. Tuples are immutable, and usually contain a heterogeneous sequence of elements that are accessed via unpacking (see later in this section) or indexing (or even by attribute in the case of namedtuples). Lists are mutable, and their elements are usually homogeneous and are accessed by iterating over the list.

One quick observation:
Currently the `dup-fixtures` command always return `0` as it's exit code, even when duplicated fixtures are found.
The main reason for this is that it's still in a very experimental fase, i.e. something could be falsely reported, and it'd break some builds out there.

If I may suggest you something, for now I'd recommend only using the `dead-fixtures` command in your build step, and add the `dup-fixtures` in some manually executed step, like your `Makefile`, `pre-commit` config and something like that.

That's true, and it's definitely a shame that this situation would upset all future tests.

However - in principle, at least - clearing the data could also be considered a "bad state" for some future migrations if they assume certain things do exist from previous migrations. (I wouldn't describe doing that as "best practice" necessarily, but I'm sure it will happen.) So if `MigrationTest` did the data-wipe before running migrations in tearDown, this could trip-up migrations that would work otherwise. (Hope that made sense, if not I'll knock together an example.)

So I think I'm inclined to say that it would be more surprising (and harder to workaround) if `MigrationTest` were to do the data-wipe. We could add it as an optional switch though I think.

You can probably just do something like:

```python
class MigrationFailTestCase(MigrationTest):

    def test_fail_hard(self):
        # put data in bad state
        MyModel.objects.create(state='bad')

        with self.assertRaises(Exception):
            self.run_migration()

        # put data back into sane state, eg:
        MyModel.objects.all().delete()
```

or maybe even

```python
class MigrationFailTestCase(MigrationTest)
    def tearDown(self):
        call_command('flush')
        super().tearDown()
```

would work for your case.

See what [`TransactionTestCase`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/test/testcases.py#L907) does which is a bit more involved, but caters for things like multiple databases.

Hope that's helpful.

data <- mx.symbol.Variable("data")

Yes, unfortunately libjpeg-turbo version available in Ubuntu package compiles turbojpeg only as a static library (turbojpeg.a) instead of dynamic library (turbojpeg.so). You need to compile libjpeg-turbo from source. For system-wide installation you can use this:
```
apt-get install autoconf automake libtool nasm
JPEG_TURBO_VERSION=1.5.2 && \
wget -q -O - https://github.com/libjpeg-turbo/libjpeg-turbo/archive/${JPEG_TURBO_VERSION}.tar.gz | tar -xzf - && \
cd libjpeg-turbo-${JPEG_TURBO_VERSION} && \
autoreconf -fiv && \
./configure --enable-shared --prefix=/usr 2>&1 >/dev/null && \
make -j"$(nproc)" install 2>&1 >/dev/null && \
rm -rf libjpeg-turbo-${JPEG_TURBO_VERSION}
```
Then you need to set `USE_LIBJPEG_TURBO_PATH` in `config.mk` to `/usr`

Solution described by Ben Darnell on Stackoverflow ["Thread local pyzmq ioloop"](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/30779019/thread-local-pyzmq-ioloop?rq=1) :

> 
> IOLoop.current() has some quirks for historical reasons. It will refer to the thread-local event loop if one exists, but it will not create one, instead falling back to the singleton (main-thread) loop.
> 
> The solution is to create a new loop in w.__init__() (self.loop = ioloop.IOLoop()) and make it current in w.run() (self.loop.make_current(). loop.start() will call make_current() for you).

Thanks @bdarnell !

Below is a simple test of `REQ_RELAXED`. In my hands, it works fine.

First, run a simple REP "echo server":
```python
import zmq
c = zmq.Context()
s = c.socket(zmq.REP)
s.bind('tcp://127.0.0.1:5555')
while True:
    s.send(s.recv())
```

Second, run this REQ client, which performs an out-of-order send:
```python
import zmq
c = zmq.Context()
s = c.socket(zmq.REQ)
s.REQ_RELAXED = True
s.REQ_CORRELATE = True
s.connect('tcp://127.0.0.1:5555')

s.send(b'test1')
print(s.recv())

s.send(b'test2')
# note: no recv after this send!

s.send(b'test3')
print(s.recv())
```

Using pyzmq version 17.0.0 and libzmq version 4.2.3 (i.e. `zmq.VERSION` is `40203`), the above works fine. However, if `REQ_RELAXED` is not set (i.e. comment out that line), then an error is raised. 

How does your test code differ? Does the above test work on your system? If not, what is the libzmq version?

I think the main question (in my mind) is if the developer *needs* configurable protocols or if there is just additional protocols that bleach should accept as allowed by default. If there are use cases where you need arbitrary protocols (I think mobile phones might work by having each app register a unique protocol used to open that app up?) then I think it needs to be configurable since there is no way to enumerate all possible protocols that a user of bleach may want to use. If the use cases are simply "here's some additional safe protocols that should be allowed" then I think the way forward would be to just add some more protocols to the list of allowed protocols.

@AndreasMalecki There were a couple of thoughts on severely limiting the `ALLOWED_PROTOCOLS`, but I think the only concern was making sure we mark this clearly as a backwards incompatible change. I wrote up another issue to address making that clear.

Given that, I think we should limit them. I think for now we should go with:

```
ALLOWED_PROTOCOLS = [u'http', u'https', u'mailto']
```

Having said that, I'm still curious about situations where this isn't great, so I'll spend some time talking with people I know who use bleach and see what they think. If anything comes out of that, I'll write up an issue and PR to fix the list.

I also like the pattern of call backs that re-install them selves to be persistent, but am not sure of all of the consequences of that design.

I suspect your approach is to ensure epoch seconds values are sufficiently large to prevent variables from going negative inside the `tzlocal` implementation?  My reaction:

- I think `parse_timestamp()` should either return an *accurate* datetime instance or raise an exception.  I don't think it should silently change the timestamp (which will happen if you "clamp" it like that).
- I think `parse_timestamp()` should not raise an exception for any timestamp obtained from an AWS service, including the local dynamo service (barring possible service bugs).  I'm no AWS expert, so maybe there are conditions under which a service can return some crazy values.  I am assuming here, possibly incorrectly, that AWS services return "reasonable" values for timestamps.  I suspect that means it should at least succeed for any non-negative epoch seconds value.
- The above two bullets should hold no matter which time zone a user is in.  Note that `time.timezone` is negative in many parts of the world (I checked the Python docs), so the above fix doesn't ensure a sufficiently large value in all parts of the world.  It could still go negative.  So I don't think it works.
- If it did work, hypothetically speaking, I think it would be more concise and understandable to use Python's built-in `max()` function, e.g. `value = max(time.timezone * 2, value)`.  My 2¢ :)

`aws:kms` encryption is specified on the `CreateMultipartUpload` operation and not on each part. Amazon S3 currently supports `aws:kms` SSE encryption for the following operations: `PutObject`, `CreateMultipartUpload`, `CopyObject`, POST object (e.g., form style requests). Specifying `aws:kms` encryption on the `UploadPart` operation is invalid as it is not an accepted value.

There is an issue in that we do not support  `ServerSideEncryption` for `UploadPart`. However, `AES256` is the only supported option for `ServerSideEncryption` for the `UploadPart` operation: see http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/KMSUsingRESTAPI.html. I'll work on fixing this, but it won't fix you bucket policy issue.

I'm not sure it's possible based on this document (http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/using-with-s3-actions.html), but you'll need to modify your bucket policy to only enforce KMS encryption on Initiate Multipart Upload operations. As far as getting this work via a policy to enforce KMS on all multi-part uploads, I suggest starting a forum thread on the Amazon S3 developer forums so that you can interact directly with S3 engineers to see if there is a workaround or if they will consider it for a feature request: https://forums.aws.amazon.com/forum.jspa?forumID=24

- With Python 2 paths and the like are supposed to be ``str`` (bytes), but the API should accept text (unicode) too.

- With Python 3 paths and the like are supposed to be ``str`` (unicode), but should accept bytes too
(decoded as utf-8).

Basically pygit2 tries to be a good Python 2 citizen and good Python 3 citizen, which develops a kind of
schizophrenia.

At the C level, the relevant line is at ``src/repository.c:100``:

    if (!PyArg_ParseTuple(args, "s", &path))

In Python 2 this is interpreted as bytes or unicode. In Python 3 this only accepts unicode.

The custom remote initialization callback is there to set the configuration for the remote which `git_clone()` will use. Only the `git_remote` object is returned to libgit2. Anything you set on the python wrapper will not be propagated back. There is in fact nowhere to store it in the remote.

The functions you can override in the remote are kept in the python object so they will only be useful for `Remote.fetch()` and `Remote.push()`; clone can only make use of the options passed in via the function call itself.

This asymmetry is unfortunate, and it comes down to the old way in which libgit2 made you set the callbacks. I've been meaning to update how we handle this by using a dictionary or some form of object which can implement an interface but time is not plentiful.

I'm thinking that a totally new approach to handling the MANIFEST.in file is the answer.

The current approach (inherited from distutils) is to recursively list all the files, bringing the whole lot into memory, and then apply MANIFEST.in commands to that list. This is the problem, and the fix is "don't do that".

It is just the wrong approach. It gives you a small speedup in one case - when you have very few files, and you end up processing MANIFEST.in directives that would cause multiple walks over the directory structure. However, in this case, OS caching means that you the second walk is usually going to be very fast.

A far better approach is to just handle MANIFEST.in directives directly - for example, a `recursive-include foo *.py` would just cause the foo directory to be recursively walked looking for `*.py` files. This sounds 'dumb', but in reality is going to beat the current approach 99.99% of the time, and by a massive factor in many cases.

There are two ways to go about this:

1) Create a class that inherits from `distutils.filelist.FileList` and then change the implementation to not use `allfiles` at all, but have `include_pattern` and `exclude_pattern` search the filesystem directly. This will break whenever other code assumes the current implementation of `FileList` e.g. the existence of the `allfiles` attribute. setuptools does this in one place, it would also have to be fixed, I think it should be possible relatively easily. (The offending code is setuptools.command.egg_info.manifest_maker._add_egg_info, and I think the fix is actually to remove the whole of the function and the one place it is called. It seems that the entire function is actually a workaround for the fact that `include_pattern` scans not the filesystem but the internally buffered version of the filesystem). However, there might be other issues that crop up.

2) Start from scratch, with a test-driven approach to implementing MANIFEST.in directive parsing.It should be possible to do this relatively easily - the language is not that complicated. Further, it shouldn't be that hard to do it with plenty of unit tests etc., especially if the code which actually walks the file system is done using a separate class for which a mock version can be supplied.

My suggestion is that this should be implemented within setuptools. If successful, it could be submitted to distutils etc. as a replacement implementation.

Hi,

1. If the producer encounters an error, it will [log it as a warning](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/producer.py#L157-L175) or in some cases [raise an exception](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/producer.py#L194). The producer handles retrying failed messages, so you can simply produce inside of a `try...except` like this:
```
try:
    producer.produce(messages)
except ProduceFailureError:
    deal_with_error()
```

2. When you call `consumer.stop()`, the consumer's fetch worker thread is [halted](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L272-L273). This means that it will stop making requests to the kafka server for more messages. However, it is possible (and likely) that after a call to `stop()`, the consumer's internal queues will still contain messages that were fetched before `stop()` was called. Thus, you will still be able to call `consumer.consume()` until the queues are empty. If you want to be certain that you've consumed all of the messages that have been fetched, you can set the `consumer_timeout_ms` kwarg to some small number greater than zero and call `consume(block=True)` 
until it returns `None`.

3. I'm not certain what you mean in this question. The `BalancedConsumer` is the only type of pykafka consumer that registers itself in ZooKeeper. If you want to consume a topic with multiple processes, you can use several `BalancedConsumers` (one in each process) all sharing the same `consumer_group` argument. See the documentation on `BalancedConsumer` [here](http://pykafka.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/balancedconsumer.html).

@zhynxn There is actually no need to gracefully disconnect the `KafkaClient` since it doesn't maintain a persistent connection. It communicates with the kafka server on a request-response basis, so every time a function on `KafkaClient` returns, it's safe to deallocate the object.

We've long wanted to redo how we were handling services, and knew the right time to do it would be when we were adding support for systemd.  There are some things in here I like.

The classes `init_system_systemd` and `init_system_sysV` are on the right track. We would need to add one for upstart, too.  I find the abstraction of these classes done by `init_system` to be unnecessary, though. I think where we would like to go with this is to have the assignment of the init_system "handler" be done when the connection to the remote node is made.  e.g. here: https://github.com/ceph/ceph-deploy/blob/master/ceph_deploy/hosts/__init__.py#L71.

There would be logic in each module's `choose_init` call that either returns an instance of `init_system_{system}` or a name saying the init type, then we create an instance of the class.  In this way, calls for all hosts can perhaps be done with: `distro.init.enable()` and `distro.init.start()` instead of the current "if systemd: do this, else: do that".

However, perhaps this is too simplistic for systemd, as there is the complication of creating service names like `ceph@osd` or however those are constructed (and I see you did some work to handle).  I think it may still be able to be done that way, however.

The other way we have tossed around doing this is instead of having the rgw create details be in `ceph_deploy/rgw.py`, we make it look more like `mon` is done, with each host module having a `rgw` directory (like `hosts/centos/rgw`) so that we can put more distro specific info in there, rather than having lots of if/else statements. It's not just BTrees, it's *any* reference to the object. References to persisted objects all include the type so that they can be unghosted:
```pycon
>>> from ZODB import DB, MappingStorage
>>> from persistent import Persistent
>>> import transaction
>>> db = DB(MappingStorage.MappingStorage())
>>> class X(Persistent): pass
>>> class Y(Persistent): pass
>>> x = X()
>>> conn = db.open()
>>> conn.root()['x'] = x
>>> transaction.commit()
>>> x.__class__ = Y
>>> type(x)
 __main__.Y
>>> x._p_changed = True
>>> transaction.commit()
>>> conn.root()['x']
<__main__.Y at 0x10d3c18c0>
>>> new_conn = db.open()
>>> new_conn.root()['x']
<__main__.X at 0x10d3c9f50>
```

In general you can't change the `__class__` of a persistent object and expect it to be consistently unghosted as the new class unless you can update all the references to it.

This is documented [here](https://github.com/zopefoundation/ZODB/blob/master/src/ZODB/serialize.py#):

> Because the persistent object reference forms include class
information, it is not possible to change the class of a persistent
object for which this form is used.  If a transaction changed the
class of an object, a new record with new class metadata would be
written but all the old references would still use the old class.

Depends on what you consider a streaming function, but I think you're correct.

If the size is a pointer, you can do something like

```c
BROTLI_BOOL BrotliEncoderFinishStream(
    BrotliEncoderState* state, size_t* encoded_size, uint8_t encoded_buffer[*encoded_size]);
```

Which should work as expected.  The length can even be an expression like `*encoded_size * sizeof(uint64_t)` if you want (you don't in this case, just wanted to point out that it's pretty flexible).

However, if you actually want to modify the array pointer itself (like you do for `BrotliEncoderCompressStream`, then you can't use a real array; it would have to be a `uint8_t**`.  Something like

```c
BROTLI_BOOL BrotliEncoderCompressStream(
    BrotliEncoderState* s, BrotliEncoderOperation op,
    size_t* restroct available_in, const uint8_t (* restrict next_in)[*available_in],
    size_t* restrict available_out, uint8_t (* restrict next_out)[*available_out],
    size_t* restrict total_out);
```

Is tempting, but it would break because an attempt to change the value of `*next_in` or `*next_out` would fail.  So, assuming that's what you mean by the streaming functions, yeah, unfortunately that won't work :(.

Basically, anywhere in brotli you have a `uint8_t**` parameter, I don't think you'll be able to use an array.  The good news is you can use `restrict` there, though…

```c
BROTLI_BOOL BrotliEncoderCompressStream(
    BrotliEncoderState* s, BrotliEncoderOperation op,
    size_t* restrict available_in, const uint8_t * restrict * restrict next_in,
    size_t* restrict available_out, uint8_t * restrict * restrict next_out,
    size_t* restrict total_out);
```

Add in the fact that you'll really want to use `BROTLI_ARRAY_PARAM`/`BROTLI_RESTRICT` (or whatever you want to call it) macros, and I think it's pretty clear why so few people bother to properly use conformant array parameters and/or restrict.

I think you may be hitting the limits of the ability to cross-compile across gcc versions.  From what I've gathered, this seems to be a bug in gcc.  cf the following links:

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35649772/undefined-reference-to-symbol-throw-out-of-range
https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=745372

Here's another tack that might work.  What if you delete the libstdc++ (and similar) libraries in the anaconda directory and add symlinks to the ones in your gcc folder?  I think all the old APIs are included in the new files, so I *think* it wouldn't break any of the Anaconda linkages.  But it would prevent these problems from having two different stdc++ libraries.

Another idea: you could try copying out (or symlinking) the libpython.so file to another directory outside of the anaconda one and tell GalSim about that (with EXTRA_LIB_PATH).  Then when GalSim includes that directory for linking to libpython.so, it wouldn't also sweep in all the other kruft that Anaconda ships.

Yes, this is non-trivial. Creating a new DyNet memory allocator that just wraps a system call to `malloc` is easy. It would result in a speed reduction, but if it is not the default, but an option I think this is reasonable.

The second thing to do would be ensuring that garbage collection is done when there are no existing remaining pointers to the memory block in use. This could basically be done by having an overridable delete operation for memory blocks, where the current behavior of "no-op" could be maintained, but if we were using the new memory efficient allocator then it does the appropriate reference counting and deletion when the reference count reaches zero.

One other concern is complexity: one of the nice things about DyNet is that it is not overly complex and bloated. We'd have to make sure that the changes can be made without making too many compromises with respect to simplicity.

So yes, this is non-trivial, but if it seems to be workable I'm happy to help you guys with the design @davidweichiang @aargueta2 (@redpony might also be interested)

> I've always been in favor a radar_position dictionary like instrument_parameters that would hold this as well as altitude, altitude_agl, others(?). The reason I bring this up is that adding a latitude and longitude field will conflict with the current top level parameters. An alternative would be to add it to fields, but that doesn't seem like the correct place. So where to add these variables?

Nearly all the dictionaries off the Radar class match one to one with those specified in the CfRadial format.  Introducing a radar_position dictionary breaks this mapping so I would be in favor of a different methods of storing the latitude and longitudes of the radar gates, maybe gate_latitude and gate_longitude attribute dictionaries which are initially None?  These could also be defined as properties like **rays_per_sweep** which is calculated on access. This may be non-ideal since calculating the locations of all gates can be a bit computationally demanding and we may not want to do it more than once. Providing a function which returns arrays of gate latitudes and longitudes avoids this but puts the burden of keeping track of these values on the user.

I provided some comments on the implementation.

I think a GCloud-specific wrapper for 3LO is relatively low priority, since
there is no user-owned data at stake, so you would typically just use it as
a way to reusing the user's permissions.

One thing that may not be widely known yet is that you can actually now use
the GCloud SDK for this. As of a release in late 2014, the Cloud SDK's
"gcloud auth login" command will also set up the Application Default
Credentials in the well-known-file location and it includes all Cloud API
scopes. This means you can use 3LO with your own credentials as a proxy at
development time, but you don't need to have an any actual 3LO related UI
in you application. We'd love to get feedback about whether this works for
scenarios like this.

That being said, I do belive there is a need for better 3L0 libraries,
primarily for APIs like that DO represent end user data (e.g. Calendar,
GMail). Ultimately I think we should  provide a better wrapped
implementation of 3LO in oauth2client, such that writing code such as that
in this change list bcomes 95% UI logic only a few library calls instead of
needed this embedded auth and serialization logic.

The attack based on the complexity of key space is not keysize but size of password space which is much more smaller if we can get key from password easily.

So, it's not necessary to break AES or revert a hash function. A possible attack may work like this:

0. Assumption: We can identify few shadowsocks packets and we have one packet contains `IV`. And it's using a weak password. 
1. enumerate possible original password `P`
2. calc traffic key K by using `EVP_BytesToKey(P)`, get IV from packet
3. decrypt packets and check theirs validity

MD5 is fast (also encryption algorithms are fast), so the step 2 and 3 can be done pretty fast. And attackers can use precomputed table, GPU and ASIC to speed it up. 

But I think for someone who use 8 digits password, their is no way to protect them...

awesome to have you working on this @Eric-Arellano! based on what I've seen floating around for Python 3.7.x perf improvements (with 3.7.0 just released last week and 3.7.1 expected in July), its possible that this effort could lead to a significant runtime speedup once pants can run seamlessly on 3.7.x. so it'd be super interesting to post some early benchmark numbers for various pants invocations (e.g. `time ./pants list ::`) once we get to an initially running state (even in a PR branch).

one quick thought on the path forward for both 2.7 + 3.x testing is that running the entirety of CI against both interpreter versions will effectively ~double the CI runtime (which is already quite lengthy at around an ~hour and a half). given that added cost, it could make sense to hasten a jump to 3.x as the "recommended" python version for pants with a strategy that could look something like:

1) get all at major consumers onboard for an eager switch to 3.x (potentially dangling the aforementioned perf #'s as carrot) as soon as this is dogfoodable
2) announce deprecation of python 2.7 support as of some two-major-versions-away target release, after which python 2.7 execution will fail (we could even assert a required minimum version at this point for safety)
3) run CI in a dual-mode config for the next two major releases - possibly as sidecar (e.g. different github org) like we've done in the past for osx to avoid CI taking 3h per run
4) kill 2.7 in CI after the deprecation release and recommend/assert a requirement for 3.x (ideally 3.7) as the defacto interpreter going forward

from then on, we could then begin to utilize 3.x features (like e.g. [data classes](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0557/)) in pants' codebase with the need for 2.7 compatibility behind us. we could also peel away the `six`/`future` compatibility layer in favor of idiomatic 3.x code at that point as well.

In terms of blockers, I believe there is at least one known issue to solve related to pants' execution interpreter vs target interpreters for working with python targets (which is currently biting us for 2.x -> 3.x IIRC) - so it'd be good to sync up with @CMLivingston on that so we can all be working strategically to unblock the initial dogfooding.

Having a latent style by a particular name doesn't prevent you from defining a style with the same name. That's exactly what Word does the first time you apply a style that isn't defined yet. The latent styles (in addition to any defined styles that are not also latent) determines what shows up in the list to pick from in the Word UI. But if you apply a latent style that isn't yet defined *in that document*, Word defines it there based on some built-in presets. That way the document doesn't have to carry around a lot of style definitions it doesn't use.

Short story is you can just define "List Bullet" and then use it. You can either define it in the starting document using Word, or use the `python-pptx` API to define it. To define it using Word, just apply that style to a new paragraph and then delete that paragraph. The paragraph goes away, but the style remains.

For what its worth, using PNG should generally still be quite efficient, because of the kind of compression that is used is very good at efficiently encoding the large areas of background that most plots have. You could try using JPEG if your plots contain images or if your plots have a lot of content.

If the movies that you make are meant to be viewed as a movie, you can also consider using ffmpeg to save as mp4. The compression is very good, and also uses the temporal dimension (i.e. it makes use of the fact that two subsequent frames are very similar). Though when pausing such a movie the quality of the viewed frame might be less good.

Wait, you are mixing something up here. `event.request.headers.get('Server')` is not going to work because `Server` is a response header and you are trying to find it in the `fetch` event's request headers. What I am suggesting here is when you make request to the server from the SW, add a custom request header that looks like `X-ServiceWorker: serviceWorker.js:v1` and on the server-side check if this header is missing then you would know that the request is not initiated by a an SW, hence do the needful (i.e., return a response that installs SW).

So this is part 1, that actually only records the subscribe/register options, right?

From looking at the change, this looks good. We should make a conscious decision about these alternatives though:

a) in the decorator, not bind `**options`, but add a 2nd positional arg that needs to be `SubscribeOptions` or `RegisterOptions` respectively
b) inside the decorator recording, not record a pair with 2nd item `**options`, but record instances of `SubscribeOptions` or `RegisterOptions`.

I agree that it should be possible to do what you want, but right now there currently isn't a way, unless you specifically call out each element in the list, i.e. `[[0][], [1][]]`

What's going on here is that the precedence of the flatten operator `[]` is really low.  So normally in a wildcard expression you'd have:  `left-hand-side[*]right-hand-side`, e.g `foo[*].bar`.  However it's possible for both the LHS and RHS to be empty: `[*].bar`.  `foo[*]`, which internally parse as: `Wildcard(empty, Field('bar'))`, and `Wildcard(Field('foo'), empty)` respectively.  It's also possible for both LHS and the RHS to be empty, `[*] -> Wildcard(empty, empty)`.

With the flatten operator, what's happening is both the RHS and LHS of the projection are being parsed as empty because the flatten operator has such a low precedence binding, and the whole result is being flattened, i.e `Flatten(Wildcard(empty, empty))`.  In order to get what you want, we'd need to be able to parse to `Wildcard(empty, Flatten())` which isn't possible right now.  Perhaps there's some syntax we can introduce to make this possible.

btw, if you're curious, you can see the AST these expressions parse to using the `jp.py --ast` option, which is included when you pip install jmespath.

Things that need to be done for this:

#### New CLI option

First, new CLI option `--genesis` which expects to be passed the filesystem path to a valid genesis JSON file.  When `--genesis` has been provided we will **require** that `--data-dir` is also specified to avoid accidental collision with the `mainnet` and `ropsten` directories until we sort this API out a bit.

#### Validation of genesis data

Next we need validation for the genesis JSON.  A simple function which takes the parsed JSON and raises an `eth_utils.ValidationError` if it is not valid.

#### Update the `ChainConfig` class

Next the `trinity.config.ChainConfig` class needs to be updated to include this genesis data.  This includes:

- New attribute `chain_id` that needs to be set on the `ChainConfig` class and probably stored on whatever `Node` class is used.

#### Setting genesis header

Next, in `trinity.chains.get_chaindb_manager` and `trinity.chains.initialize_database` we need to setup the database.  We'll need the `chain_class` to do this.  When `--genesis` has been specified, we need to use `chain_class.from_genesis(...)` to initialize the data dir.  Ideally, we should be able to convert how the *mainnet* and *ropsten* chains are initialized to use the same mechanism, maintaining a local copy of the mainnet and ropsten genesis JSON (and probably validating them against the genesis header values from the py-evm package).

Part of this is going to be dynamically configuring the `vm_configuration` for the chain class from the provided genesis data.

What version of Conda are you using? 
This does not happen in the recent versions of conda.  `conda update conda` will update your conda to most recent version. 

```
conda install nltk qtpy
Fetching package metadata ...........
Solving package specifications: .

Package plan for installation in environment /Users/tkim/miniconda3:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    qtpy: 1.2.1-py27_0

The following packages will be SUPERSEDED by a higher-priority channel:

    nltk: 3.2.2-py27_0 conda-forge --> 3.2.2-py27_0

Proceed ([y]/n)? n
```

I could be wrong, but I don't think this was created by conda 4.4.0rc1

This directory could probably have been created by conda-build. 
https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/3.0.11/conda_build/build.py#L169-L176

This was removed in conda-build v3.0.12
Regarding the original issue, I think we've solved this with ruamel_yaml.  It's been out for a while now, although I think we just recently wired it into conda-env (guess we missed it before).

I think there are two issues being conflated here. 

One is related to the `channels` section in the above example. That is fixed by avoiding the ruamel_yaml types or otherwise handling them correctly. 

The other issue is the `channel::package` syntax. The `::` syntax necessitates fixing the regex in conda, no? I haven't seen that syntax in my environments and don't know how it gets produced. I just always prefer the first place a package is found as defined by the order of the channels.

Yes use miniconda 3 or anaconda 3 in your base env or use Linux it macOS.

Process elevation is achieved with a subprocess because Windows can't actually promote a process.  It has to be run elevated from the beginning of its lifetime.  Everything that "elevates" itself is actually creating a new process.  In this code, it is achieved on https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/1862/files#diff-ba947b3623b8588707b511f76ade5112R53

Note that on Windows, elevation and identity are two separate concepts: you must be an administrator to elevate, but administrators are not by definition elevated.  This differs from Unix, where identity/group carries all of the concepts of elevated permissions.

Hi @fxdgear, here is my code (simplified for example purpose):
```
ELK_CONN_SETTING = {
    'use_ssl': True,
   'verify_certs': False,
    'ca_certs': os.path.join(CERTS_DIR, 'HTTPS_servers.pem'),
    'client_cert': os.path.join(CERTS_DIR, 'host.pem'),
    'client_key': os.path.join(CERTS_DIR, 'host.key')
}

es = elasticsearch.Elasticsearch(
    [server.hostname],
    **ELK_CONN_SETTING
)
```
I am using this code with `elasticsearch-5.5.1`, to connect with an Elasticsearch server with version `5.6.4`.

However, reading the code of tag `5.5.1` or branch `5.5.x` of this library you will notice that If you set `verify_certs=False` the two parameters `client_cert` and `client_key` wont be added to `kw`, and so will be ignored:
```
if verify_certs:
    if not ca_certs:
        raise ImproperlyConfigured("Root certificates are missing for certificate "
            "validation. Either pass them in using the ca_certs parameter or "
            "install certifi to use it automatically.")

    kw.update({
        'cert_reqs': 'CERT_REQUIRED',
        'ca_certs': ca_certs,
        'cert_file': client_cert,
        'key_file': client_key,
    })
else:
   # NOR cert_file NOR key_file will be passed to kw
   # and they will just be ignored
    warnings.warn(
        'Connecting to %s using SSL with verify_certs=False is insecure.' % host)
```
So, in versione `5.5.1` and previous of `elastichsearch-py`, it is not possible to connect to an Elasticsearch server with a self-signed certificate using SSL client auth.

This could be fine for production environment, but may be a pain in a development environment.

The error is indicating your Root CA is missing from the verification cert. The verification cert needs to be signed by the root CA and contain the verification key given to you in the subject.

You can look at how [certGen.sh](https://github.com/Azure/azure-iot-sdk-c/blob/master/tools/CACertificates/certGen.sh) was implemented to get an idea how to do this with openssl (or the equivalent Powershell code and dependencies).

Unless there's a large number of files being touched, the individual hook executables will usually only be called either 0 or 1 times (receiving the filenames as positional arguments) -- it uses xargs so if there's a ton of filenames it'll end up calling your executable multiple times.  This is documented here: http://pre-commit.com/#passing-arguments-to-hooks

As for enforcing a hook that always runs exactly once, you could probably use the "migration mode" approach documented here: http://pre-commit.com/#running-in-migration-mode .  In migration mode, pre-commit will run whatever existing script was sitting at .git/hooks/pre-commit when `pre-commit install` was executed *before* running all the other hooks.

The migration mode is a bit clunky (doesn't have nice printing, doesn't do the stash behaviour, doesn't honor merge conflicts, etc.) so when we've reached for something similar to what you're asking, we've instead written hooks that simply ignore the passed arguments and are less concerned about them running more than once (or better, make them idempotent!).  For this we've essentially made the argparser ignore positional arguments when executing.

the env var that needs to be set would specify the entrypoint of the kernel launcher, so in terms of concerns it'd be part of the "kernel configuration" (i.e. something we set only at kernel launch time vs something we'd want as a static env var in the parent, which in theory could potentially leak into other non-desired contexts or kernel launches). tho it seems already possible to embed a static env var like this directly into a `kernel.json` - so really the remaining gap is the self-reference bit (i.e. understanding and being able to parameterize the values of `sys.executable` and `sys.argv[0]` from the running notebook server context).

so afaict, to make this all first class it seems like jupyter would need a way to specify kernel configuration in a plugin type model (i.e. executable python code vs json). it might also be cool to use a registry/discovery type pattern against the installed plugins so that just e.g. their presence in the python environment could enable them for use. this would make it as easy as a `pip install` to add new kernel types.

fwiw, I've posted an initial implementation of the surrogate shim approach described above [here](https://gist.github.com/kwlzn/e5282ed81a95da9935614de3c136c1c7) which is working well for the moment.

Wow, nice catch and nice code.

I ran these and only one gave a divide-by-zero error:

```python
print grad(np.power)(1., 0.)
print grad(np.power)(0., 1.)
print grad(np.power)(0., 0.)

print grad(np.power, 1)(1., 0.)
print grad(np.power, 1)(0., 1.)  # divide by zero error!
print grad(np.power, 1)(0., 0.)
```

Since the derivative is with respect to argument index 1, the line of code is actually [the next one](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/3336f549c5164a52d0c94654ea912920468055e2/autograd/numpy/numpy_grads.py#L78). The problem is that we're evaluating `x * anp.log(x)` when `x` is zero, so we get a divide-by-zero error in evaluating the log.

I think a good fix is to replace `anp.log(x)` with `anp.log(anp.where(x, x, 1.))`, though let me know if there's something more slick to do.

I'll push that fix plus a test that covers the issue. Please reopen if I missed things!

Hi @yipcma, thank you for opening an issue!

I think creating string representations of the objects is best considered on a case-by-case basis by implementers. If we were to do this in the client itself, it would probably look something like this:

```python
def __str__(self):
    return self.__dict__.__str__()
```

This would however make it very appealing and easy to simply log that without much thought, but some objects - like messages - may, for some use-cases, contain very sensitive data (think: 2FA codes, password reset links...)
That is of course a pretty serious security issue. Not having a `__str__` implementation on these objects triggers people to think a bit more about what to log.

Hopefully you understand this concern, and thanks again for your suggestion!

i5513 <notifications@github.com> writes:

> Then I don't understand how sshuttle work
> I was thinking on the next tcp connections:
> my computer <=sshuttle=> jump server <=> XIV Storage

This is absolutely correct. There are two completely independant TCP
connections involved.

The sshuttle server pretends to have the same IP address as XIV Storage.

So "my computer" will have a TCP connection IP(XIV Stoage) and receive
packets from IP(XIV Storage). In actual fact sshuttle terminates this
connection, not XIV Storage. sshuttle is acting like a transparent
proxy.

sshuttle will relay packets to/from the 2nd TCP connection. This is real
TCP connection. This is the TCP connection between the "jump server" and
the "XIV Storage" and will behave exactly like a normal TCP connection
would.

The two TCP connections are completely independent, and have nothing in
common. Despite the fact they use the same IP address.

Does this help?

Ok, the script ends up with something like:

    In order to upload packages run the following command:
    + echo twine upload /tmp/le.0.0.0.dev20151025.YLl/dist.0.0.0.dev20151025/*/*
    twine upload /tmp/le.0.0.0.dev20151025.YLl/dist.0.0.0.dev20151025/*/*

Twine uses the following [regexp](https://github.com/pypa/twine/blob/master/twine/wheel.py#L34) to extract Python version:

    wheel_file_re = re.compile(
        r"""^(?P<namever>(?P<name>.+?)(-(?P<ver>\d.+?))?)
            ((-(?P<build>\d.*?))?-(?P<pyver>.+?)-(?P<abi>.+?)-(?P<plat>.+?)
            \.whl|\.dist-info)$""",
        re.VERBOSE)

and:

    def py_version(self):
        wheel_info = wheel_file_re.match(self.filename)
        return wheel_info.group("pyver")

A filename like `/tmp/le.0.0.0.dev20151025.YLl/dist.0.0.0.dev20151025/letsencrypt-nginx/letsencrypt_nginx-0.0.0.dev20151025-py2-none-any.whl` triggers a bug in twine (there is a hyphen in `letsencrypt-nginx` folder name).

`os.path.basename` would be a sufficient solution; I don't really know why twine uses full paths.

On the other hand, you should  stop using dashes in package names. Even underscores are not recommended by [PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#id32).

This is a tricky situation, because the API is pretty clear about the fact that it's going to check your local time zones for valid zone names to parse. The real issue is that this is really fuzzy and context-dependent behavior. I'd love to deprecate it at some point in the future and make it a default-off option.

That said, I don't think this should be removed as is, because it would actually remove existing functionality with no way to replicate the old behavior, whereas currently you can get your desired behavior this way:

```python
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil.parser import parse
from dateutil.tz import tzutc


d1 = datetime(2002, 10, 2, 13, 0, tzinfo=tzutc())
d2 = parse('Wed, 02 Oct 2002 13:00:00 GMT', tzinfos={'GMT': tzutc()})
print(d1 == d2)
```

This is generally advisable, because if you notice, RFC822 also specifies 'EST', 'EDT', and a number of other zones, none of which can be parsed _at all_ by `dateutil.parser` unless they are the local zone, (and never will be) for ambiguity reasons. The real lesson is that you need to provide the context yourself if you know it.

That said, there is _one_ possible tweak that I think I would be comfortable with, which is that we can improve the time zone parser logic such that when it decides to assign `tzlocal()` it will check if the time zone that actually gets assigned is the time zone we were expecting, e.g.: `dt.replace(tzinfo=res.tzname).tzname() == res.tzname`, and if not, see if there's another way to parse it that _does_ create a time that exists. This same logic should also be used to assign whether or not the parsed date has the `fold` attribute, but I will make a separate issue for that.

Hi @0xfeedface1993! Thanks for the image, that is really helpful since I am able to reproduce this issue. It looks like we are unable to load PNG images that are saved with an 8-bit palette (that is, a table of 256 colors). Images without color palettes are supported in both RGB and RGBA (RGB + opacity channel), but with RGBA images you can run into issues later (as in #114 with the object detector). Your proposed work-around of using `convert` is a great idea and should work. I would convert it to `'RGB'` though, to make sure you don't trigger #114. I'm really sorry for the inconvenience this is causing!

I will leave this issue open until we have a more permanent fix in place.

Ansible looks like a great tool to help us address both this issue and #31. I've walked through the [Python API](http://docs.ansible.com/ansible/developing_api.html) page on their documentation, among others. While this the documentaiton provides just enough information to get started, it's extremely shallow.

In a bid to become more familiar with Ansible's API, I cloned the Ansible source repository and started walking through code. Unfortunately, the raw source code does not reflect how Ansible is used. What do I mean by that? Here's the beginning of a Python script that uses Ansible:

```python
import ansible.runner

runner = ansible.runner.Runner(
```

This code is valid, despite the fact that there is no `Runner` class defined in Ansible, nor is there a `runner` module. The closest I can find is class `TaskExecutor` in module `lib.ansible.executor.task_executor`:

```python
class TaskExecutor:

    '''
    This is the main worker class for the executor pipeline, which
    handles loading an action plugin to actually dispatch the task to
    a given host. This class roughly corresponds to the old Runner()
    class.
    '''
```

I've been unable to find a good explanation of how the API is changing in Ansible version 2, if at all. Given that I'm left with walking through sample scripts and the source code to figure this out, perhaps the best solution is to go ahead and use Ansible 2 from the get-go. At this point, no Ansible 2 releases are available in PyPi. However, we can pop this in `setup.txt`:

```python
install_requires=['ansible>=2', …]
```

And this in `requirements.txt`:

    git+https://github.com/ansible/ansible.git@v2.0.0-0.6.rc1#egg=ansible

And we can manually update to newer tags as time goes on. Not as elegant as I'd like, but doable.

We found that we had to assign fully qualified hostnames in Ubuntu in the cluster.local domain before we installed kubeadm. For example we set hostnames to node1.cluster.local, node2.cluster.local, etc. This includes updating both the /etc/hostname and /etc/hosts files using this name. Then we installed kubeadm init and kubeadm join and all the K8s nodes now have the fully qualified hostname when you display kubectl get nodes. * ensures that python executable is installed

I'm assuming you're using the Apache plugin. The Apache plugin has to create temporary vhosts for the purposes of solving the challenge. If your curious, you can read what exactly is required by reading the relevant section the [ACME spec](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-02#section-7.3).

After creating these vhosts, Apache has to reload your web server. This is not a full restart, but a "graceful" reload. No connections should be dropped but this might be the cause of the delay you're seeing. To work around this problem, you could try to use the [webroot](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#webroot) plugin to renew your certificates.

To actually fix this problem though, I need a little more information. Would you be willing to provide the virtual hosts files you have enabled on your site? You can redact any values you want, but this would allow us to see the configuration you're using and give us more information to help diagnose the problem.

Hi!

All apio packages are installed in the isolated environment "~/.apio" to avoid conflicts with other packages in your system and to provide a package structure compatible with Windows and Mac.

If you want to use directly in your Linux system you can add it manually into the path by adding these lines in your `~/.bashrc` file:

```
export PATH=~/.apio/packages/toolchain-icestorm/bin:$PATH
export PATH=~/.apio/packages/toolchain-iverilog/bin:$PATH
export PATH=~/.apio/packages/tools-system/bin:$PATH
```

Then run `source .bashrc` to apply it. It will be automatically applied to new terminals.

Other solution is to download directly the packages and uncompress them in `/usr/local/`, `~/.local`, etc. These are the package's repositories:

* https://github.com/FPGAwars/toolchain-icestorm/releases
* https://github.com/FPGAwars/toolchain-iverilog/releases
* https://github.com/FPGAwars/tools-system/releases

Regards.

I'm definitely in favour of a way of grouping together parameters, and the ParameterNode looks like a good implementation. Also when combining this with monitoring tools etc, it will be very useful to have a the parameters structured, instead of a giant list.

The functionality of Channel/MultiChannelParameter/ChannelList seems to have a lot of overlap with the proposed ParameterNode, they both seem to be a container for parameters. In this sense, I like the name ParameterNode better, especially since parameters may want to be grouped in a way unrelated to a channel.
At the moment I can't think of a situation where a ParameterNode could not be used instead. @Dominik-Vogel You mentioned channels being more suitable as homogeneous parameters, could you give an example of it's advantage over a ParameterNode? 

@AdriaanRol enhancing the get/set functionality of instruments also sounds good. I'm thinking this could also be a feature of the ParameterNode. In this case, the ParameterNode actually also has a lot of overlap with the CombinedParameter (which is currently in need of improvements). I'm wondering if the ParameterNode would be able to take over its functionality entirely. The asterisk symbol could then act as a flattening of a ParameterNode, returning a flattened ParameterNode. For example, say we have:
```
ch1 (ParameterNode):
    in1 (ParameterNode):
        trigger_threshold (Parameter)
    in2 (ParameterNode):
        trigger_threshold (Parameter)
```

By then specifying `ch1['in*]` (or via .get or some other method), we would get a flattened ParameterNode:
```
ch1_flattened (ParameterNode):
    in1_trigger_threshold (Parameter)
    in2_trigger_threshold (Parameter)
```
We could then get/set its values as we would for a CombinedParameter (`ch1['in*'].set([1, 1.5])`)

@lanec there are two parts on this problem:

1. Authenticate users with the right permission to access Gmail API
2. Access Gmail API and grab the data you need

`python-social-auth` solves just the first point, you need to specify the permissions needed to access Gmail API, there's a full list [here](https://developers.google.com/gmail/api/auth/scopes#gmail_scopes). Just define them in the `SOCIAL_AUTH_GOOGLE_OAUTH2_SCOPE` setting and let the users authenticate with Google OAuth2. If using Google Plus, then set the values in the setting `SOCIAL_AUTH_GOOGLE_PLUS_SCOPE`.

To solve the second, you need to write the code to pull the emails and store them in your database the best way it fits your need, once you have the user authenticated, you will have an `access_token` stored in the db, using that token will grant you access to Gmail API to call any of the API endpoints (authorized by the scope selected).

Here's an snippet about how to call the Gmail API, this example just retrieves the email ids, but you can tune it to fit your case.

```python
import httplib2

from datetime import datetime

from apiclient.discovery import build
from oauth2client.client import OAuth2Credentials

from django.conf import settings

from social_django.models import UserSocialAuth

# Assuming user to be an instance of an user that authenticated through google:
usa = user.social_auth.get(provider="google-oauth2")  # or provider="google-plus"
revoke_uri = None
user_agent = 'PythonSocialAuth'

credentials = OAuth2Credentials(
    usa.extra_data['access_token'],
    settings.SOCIAL_AUTH_GOOGLE_OAUTH2_KEY,
    settings.SOCIAL_AUTH_GOOGLE_OAUTH2_SECRET,
    usa.extra_data['refresh_token'],
    datetime.fromtimestamp(usa.extra_data['auth_time']),
    revoke_uri,
    user_agent,
    scopes=settings.SOCIAL_AUTH_GOOGLE_OAUTH2_SCOPE
)

http = httplib2.Http()
http = credentials.authorize(http)
service = build('gmail', 'v1', http=http)

emails = service.users().messages().list(
    userId=usa.user.email,
    q='FROM:lane@joinjune.com'
).execute()

# emails contains a dict like:
# {'messages': [{'id': '15e34e63191fa12f', 'threadId': '15e34e63191fa12f'}],
#  'resultSizeEstimate': 1}

# Remember to set the SOCIAL_AUTH_GOOGLE_OAUTH2_SCOPE
# to any of the options listed at: https://developers.google.com/gmail/api/auth/scopes
```

```
streamlink <stream_url> best -p "vlc -I dummy stream:///dev/stdin -vvv --http-port 8080 --sout '#standard{access=http,mux=ts}'"

I'm not familiar with any Python packages which install man pages via pip and would actually be against it due to packaging standards and this being unexpected behavior. Man pages should be handled by distro packages and have specific requirements based on the distro (take the debian docs for example: https://www.debian.org/doc/debian-policy/#manual-pages).

There has also been discussion on this previously here (https://github.com/pypa/packaging-problems/issues/72). I don't really think this can of worms is worth opening. How would we handle conflicts where a user installs the package, then installs the pip version? There would be a conflict in management there which could negatively impact the user's system. In addition we provide online documentation and the `--help` option is quite extensive and follows the standards expected for a Python project.

Thanks for filing this issue. You're right that things are more complicated with threads. You'll need the lock around the output because of how the output capture works - basically, the output widget captures on the frontend any output sent with the given parent message id inside of the context handler (it literally takes over the output display mechanism for any message with that particular parent id). If you have threads all sending messages at the same time, the frontend could easily get mixed up about where output messages should go.

However, I've run the above code at least 10 times and the output looks perfect each time - all plots show up in order. Is this reliably not showing a plot for you? Does it also happen without matplotlib plots (e.g., plain text)? To really debug this, it would be very helpful to see at the actual websocket messages being sent to/from the browser (you can see these in the Chrome debugger by refreshing the page with the debugger open, go to the Networks page, and click on the appropriate websocket connection - let me know if you can't find it).

I'd be happy if we copied the continuous release tooling and process from Hypothesis: https://hypothesis.works/articles/continuous-releases/

- Every pull request that touches the source gets a release.  No exceptions.  (you may want to move or exempt tests though, and docs may but need not cause a release)
- It does the normal github status checks thing, which has worked very well in practice for Hypothesis (zero problems out of >100 PRs/releases)
- It's entirely built out of in-repo scripts, with no external bot or service.  You just click merge, and CI does its thing - which now includes releasing if the tests pass on master.

TLDR; this wouldn't scale up to CPython or Rust but it works very well for Hypothesis and I expect would for Trio too, if you like the style.

By the time being, we have logs like:
```
2018-05-24 19:06:41,541 SUMMARY: Items total read/total processed/total written: 58168/399449/399449
```
This is from areas of code, but most of gelk processes could share similar information.

From my point of view, I suggest to audit all executions in a database, in such a way we have a minimum information of what happened, let's say:
| Task | start date | end date | read | processed | written |

We can add more columns if we find more useful information we would like to have there. This way, we could query that table to know whether a given task is running properly, for instance, it is running on a daily basis, not taking so long, and number of items read and written are consistent from one day to another and even with the number of items we have in the indices.

All those checks can be done manually by an operator if needed, but I think it would be easy to do them automatically.

I will implement a service allowing tasks to log when they start and finish. The service would be pretty simple and would listen for that events. Each of those events would include all the info we want to log (dates, number of items read and written, etc.)

Just my 2 cents.

本 PR 所实现的 **函数js化** 功能从一定角度上来说，是现有 json.dumps 的扩展，可以将其与现有的相关代码作进一步整合。

- `utils.json_dumps` 函数整合至 `javascript` 模块。
- 提供统一的转化函数compile_to_javascript，将 `dumps` 和 `compile` 统一于一个函数

```
def compile_to_javascript(options, functions_list):
    options_in_json = json.dumps(data, indent=indent, cls=UnknownTypeEncoder)
    if len(functions_list): # 携带函数
        options_in_json = javascript.unescape_js_function(options_in_json)
        custom_function = javascript.compile()
    else:
        custom_function  = ''
    return options_in_json, custom_function  # 均为字符串
```

在 *engine.py* 调用示例

```
options_in_json, custom_function  = compile_to_javascript(chart.options, function)

CHART_CONFIG_FORMATTER.format(custom_function=custom_function,options=options_in_json)
```

Yes, this would be true for any/all python packages with extensions.  Python always searches the cwd first, so if you `import zmq` when there is a folder called 'zmq' in the cwd (or a zmq.py), that will be used instead of anything you may have installed.  In this case, the zmq it's importing (the zmq source tree) *is* pyzmq, but does not contain the compiled extensions (initthreads is just the first one it asks for).

If you want the source location to be a valid zmq, use `python setup.py build_ext --inplace`, which instructs Python to build the extensions in the same directory as the sources, rather than putting them in the usual build directory.
